items.q: Is the greenhouse effect itself a bad thing?
items.correct: No — the natural greenhouse effect keeps Earth warm enough to be habitable; the problem is the enhanced effect caused by extra emissions from human activity.
items.misconception: All greenhouse warming is harmful and unnatural.
items.random_wrong: The greenhouse effect is caused by glass buildings reflecting sunlight.
items.why: Students conflate the natural effect with human enhancement, weakening their explanations of climate change.

items.q: Is recycling the most effective way to manage waste?
items.correct: It helps, but reducing consumption and reusing items save more energy and resources; recycling still uses energy for collection and reprocessing.
items.misconception: As long as waste is recycled, how much we consume doesn't matter.
items.random_wrong: Recycling means burning waste to generate electricity.
items.why: Students put recycling at the top of the hierarchy; reduce and reuse rank above it.

items.correct: Not only — sustainability balances environmental protection with social fairness and economic viability, so a scheme must also work for people and economies long term.
items.misconception: Sustainability is purely about protecting nature.
items.random_wrong: Sustainable describes a building that cannot fall down.
items.why: The three-pillar view of sustainability is needed for evaluation questions on managing the environment.

items.correct: Meeting present needs without compromising future generations' ability to meet theirs
items.misconception: Maximising economic growth at any cost
items.random_wrong: Banning all use of natural resources
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Meeting present needs without compromising future generations' ability to meet theirs. “Maximising economic growth at any cost” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Which gas contributes most to the enhanced greenhouse effect from human activity?
items.why: It's Carbon dioxide. “Oxygen” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Which of these is a renewable energy source?
items.why: It's Wind. “Coal” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: In the waste hierarchy, the most preferred option is to:
items.why: It's Reduce consumption. “Recycle materials” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: A household cuts its annual electricity use from 4,000 kWh to 3,000 kWh. What is the percentage reduction?
items.why: The real figure is 25 %. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
